title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Max-severity SimpleHelp auth-bypass stays actively exploited as its federal KEV deadline lapses — the remote-support tool integrators use to reach OT is being used to drop malware
items.content_text: CISA added CVE-2026-48558, a CVSS 10.0 authentication-bypass flaw in SimpleHelp's remote monitoring and management (RMM) platform, to its KEV catalog on 29 June; the 2 July federal deadline has now lapsed while exploitation continues. On OIDC-configured servers SimpleHelp fails to verify identity-token signatures, so an unauthenticated attacker can forge a fully authenticated “Technician” session — administrative control of the server, in some configurations bypassing MFA — and attackers are already using it to drop the TaskWeaver loader and Djinn infostealer. Roughly 14,000 SimpleHelp servers are online, an estimated 1,000 directly vulnerable. Because RMM tools bridge integrators, OEMs and service providers into plant, utility and building-automation networks, a compromised server is a pre-authenticated path across the IT/OT boundary.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: KEV-added 29 Jun 2026 — federal deadline 2 Jul 2026 (lapsed); exploitation ongoing

items.title: Canada's CSE reveals a pro-Russia group claimed control-level access to a Quebec water plant — pumps, chlorine dosing and pressure allegedly within reach
items.content_text: In its 2025–2026 annual report, Canada's Communications Security Establishment (CSE) and its Canadian Centre for Cyber Security disclosed that pro-Russia hacktivist group NoName057(16) claimed it had breached an unnamed Quebec municipality's water treatment plant and gained the ability to covertly control pumps, chlorine dosing, pressure settings and the monitoring and alert systems operators rely on. CSE said it learned of the activity only through the group's own public boast — the utility did not detect it independently, which is the most telling detail. The report does not confirm whether the claimed level of control was real; pro-Russia hacktivists routinely exaggerate. But a national cyber authority treating the claim as credible enough to feature is itself the signal. Treat it as a template threat: assume internet-exposed or weakly-authenticated HMI/SCADA at small utilities is being hunted, verify no unauthorized changes to chemical-dosing or pressure setpoints, pull operator interfaces off any public path, and enforce MFA on all remote access.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CSE / Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (annual report)
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed 30 Jun 2026 — NoName claim, not confirmed

items.title: CISA/FBI/EPA/DOE: Iranian-affiliated actors keep hitting internet-exposed Allen-Bradley PLCs — with confirmed operational disruption
items.content_text: U.S. agencies continue to warn that Iranian-affiliated actors are exploiting internet-facing Rockwell Automation/Allen-Bradley programmable logic controllers across water and wastewater, energy and government-facilities sectors, a campaign running since at least March and re-emphasized amid heightened Middle East tensions. Unlike the many inflated hacktivist claims, this activity is agency-confirmed: reporting describes malicious interaction with project files, manipulation of data shown on HMI/SCADA displays, and in some cases real operational disruption and financial loss. The actors are reaching PLCs left exposed on the public internet, often protected only by default or weak credentials, rather than burning novel exploits — which makes the fix largely one of hygiene and exposure reduction. Get every PLC and OT device off the public internet or behind a VPN with MFA, change all default/shared credentials, and hunt for unexpected configuration or logic changes on internet-reachable controllers.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CISA / FBI / EPA / DOE joint alert
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Ongoing — re-warned late Jun 2026 amid Mideast tensions

items.title: Cal Water finds no OT breach after Iran-linked Handala claimed it could cut the water supply — a textbook “claimed vs confirmed” case
items.content_text: California Water Service (Cal Water), one of the largest U.S. investor-owned water utilities, investigated a breach claimed by the Iran-linked group Handala and reported finding no evidence of activity in its operational-technology environment. Handala — which presents as hacktivist but is widely assessed to be a front for Iranian state operations — asserted it could have disrupted the water supply and leaked roughly 5 GB of data. Analysts who reviewed the dump found the group had actually reached a customer billing database (containing personal data) and an internal RTKBase NTRIP GPS caster used by field crews; neither system treats or distributes water, and independent assessments say nothing in the evidence supports the shutdown claim. The episode is a clean reminder that hacktivist “we could turn off your water” claims frequently outrun the actual access — but also that Handala's toolkit reportedly includes wipers and MBR-overwriting malware, so an IT-side foothold at a utility still warrants urgent containment and a hard look at IT-to-OT pathways. Verify OT segmentation and monitoring, confirm no bridge from breached IT systems to control networks, and reset exposed credentials.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Reported late Jun 2026 — OT impact not confirmed

items.title: Sysdig documents “JADEPUFFER” — the first ransomware-style attack run end-to-end by an autonomous AI agent, from exploit to data destruction
items.content_text: Sysdig's Threat Research Team this week described what it assesses as the first extortion operation executed end-to-end by an AI agent with no human at the keyboard. The actor, dubbed JADEPUFFER, gained initial access by exploiting CVE-2025-3248 — a long-known, previously KEV-listed remote-code-execution flaw in the Langflow AI-workflow platform — on an internet-facing instance, then autonomously performed reconnaissance, stole credentials, established persistence, compromised a production database and destroyed data before demanding payment. It follows Sysdig's May case in which an LLM agent chained a fresh CVE through four internal pivots to exfiltrate a full database dump in under an hour. Why it matters for OT: the entire intrusion ran at machine speed against an unpatched, internet-exposed application — exactly the profile of the exposed HMIs, historians, remote-access portals and engineering web apps that front industrial networks — and it compresses the defender's response window from days to minutes. The mitigations are unchanged but the tempo is not: eliminate internet exposure of unpatched apps (especially anything with a KEV-listed CVE), enforce credential vaulting and egress restrictions, and tune detection for machine-speed lateral movement.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Sysdig TRT / The Hacker News / The Register

items.title: PTC Windchill RCE under active exploitation — the PLM backbone that holds engineering drawings and pivots toward the shop floor
items.content_text: On 25 June CISA added a critical improper-input-validation flaw (CVE-2026-12569, CVSS 9.3) enabling unauthenticated remote code execution in PTC Windchill PDMLink/FlexPLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Reporting indicates attackers continued dropping web shells onto unpatched, internet-reachable instances. Windchill is the product-lifecycle-management hub for automotive, aerospace, defense and other discrete manufacturers, so a compromise exposes engineering drawings, bills of material and supplier data — and gives an attacker a credible pivot toward connected OT and shop-floor systems. Treat any internet-facing Windchill/FlexPLM instance as presumed-targeted: patch immediately, hunt for unexpected JSP files and web shells, remove internet exposure, rotate credentials, and review what OT and supplier data the PLM tier can reach.

items.title: New “CitrixBleed-pattern” NetScaler flaw exploited within 24 hours of disclosure — memory leakage from the remote-access gateways many industrial sites sit behind
items.content_text: Citrix shipped fixes this week for six NetScaler ADC / NetScaler Gateway vulnerabilities, and the headline one — CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS 8.8), an insufficient-input-validation bug that causes a memory overread in the appliance's SAML handling — was being exploited in the wild less than 24 hours after public disclosure. Researchers at watchTowr showed that a malformed SAML authentication request padded with whitespace forces the parser to read past its buffer and return adjacent process memory to the client, echoing the CitrixBleed flaws whose leaked session tokens fueled major intrusions in 2023–24; honeypot operators observed live exploitation attempts beginning 30 June. Exposure requires the appliance to be configured as a SAML identity provider, which narrows the affected population but does not diminish the pattern: NetScaler gateways front VPN and remote-access services at plants, utilities and corporate networks that bridge toward OT, and leaked memory can include material that lets attackers hijack authenticated sessions. Apply the fixed builds now, confirm whether any NetScaler is configured for SAML, terminate active sessions and rotate credentials after patching, and watch authentication logs for anomalous SAML requests.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Patched late Jun 2026 — exploitation from 30 Jun, reported 1–2 Jul 2026

items.title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster pre-auth RCE now seeing exploitation attempts — the load balancer that fronts many enterprise and OT-facing services
items.content_text: eSentire's Threat Response Unit reported that CVE-2026-8037 (CVSS 9.8), a pre-authentication OS command-injection flaw in Progress Kemp LoadMaster, began drawing active exploitation attempts on 29 June — days after a public proof-of-concept and detailed write-up landed. The bug sits in the appliance's API: unsanitized input to the “/accessv2” endpoint (an out-of-bounds heap read in a string-escaping routine) lets an unauthenticated attacker inject commands and run code as root. The same defect affects other Progress ADC products (ECS Connection Manager, ObjectScale Connection Manager and the MOVEit WAF). eSentire noted the attempts it saw failed and produced no post-compromise activity, but with a working PoC circulating, successful exploitation is expected to follow. LoadMaster and ADC gear commonly sit at the network edge in front of web, VPN, remote-access and sometimes OT-facing services, so a pre-auth root takeover is a strong pivot point toward internal and control networks. Apply Progress's fixed release now, restrict management/API access, and pull the appliance off any untrusted network.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Exploitation attempts from 29 Jun 2026 — reported 1 Jul 2026

items.title: Delta Electronics DVP12SE PLC exposes unauthenticated Modbus — anyone on the network can rewrite control logic, and no patch yet
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-181-07 (30 June) warns that the Delta Electronics DVP12SE programmable logic controller exposes its Modbus TCP service on port 502 with no authentication or access control, so any reachable network source can read and write coils, holding registers, relay states and process-control functions without credentials or operator approval (CVE-2026-12818, CVE-2026-12819). A companion resource-exhaustion issue lets an attacker flood the Modbus port to degrade the device. In practice this means an attacker with network line-of-sight can remotely issue commands, change operational values and interfere with control logic — the classic insecure-by-design exposure that makes flat OT networks and internet-reachable PLCs so dangerous. All versions are affected and Delta is still working on a fix, so mitigation is compensating controls: enable the PLC's built-in IP Filter, segment and firewall the controller off any untrusted network, and never expose Modbus/502 to the internet.

items.title: Critical auth-bypass in Frangoteam FUXA web SCADA/HMI — 1,200+ instances exposed, many at water and solar sites
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-181-02 (30 June) covers CVE-2026-13207, an authentication-bypass flaw (CVSS v4 8.6) in the open-source FUXA web SCADA/HMI platform (versions 1.3.1 and earlier) caused by dot-segment path normalization in its REST API. An unauthenticated attacker can reach user accounts and role assignments, exposing the OT privilege structure to reconnaissance. Researchers at GreyNoise reported finding more than 1,200 internet-facing FUXA instances shortly after disclosure — most still unpatched and a number of them at water-treatment and solar operations, exactly the small, distributed sites that put dashboards online for remote monitoring. Frangoteam shipped a fix in version 1.3.2 and CISA released Snort/Suricata signatures. Upgrade to 1.3.2, pull FUXA off the public internet behind a VPN, rotate credentials, and load the detection signatures into your IDS/IPS.

items.title: On-prem SharePoint RCE jumps into CISA KEV after real-world attacks — the intranet backbone many plants and utilities still run in-house
items.content_text: CISA added CVE-2026-45659, a remote-code-execution flaw in on-premises Microsoft SharePoint Server (CVSS 8.8, caused by deserialization of untrusted data), to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on 1 July after confirming active exploitation, with a federal remediation deadline of 4 July. The bug lets an authenticated user holding only Site Member permissions run code on the server — notably, Microsoft had rated exploitation “less likely” when it patched the issue back in May, so any organization that deferred the fix on that basis should re-prioritize now. On-premises SharePoint is a workhorse across manufacturing, utility and engineering-firm IT, holding drawings, procedures and project data and often bridging toward OT-adjacent file shares and identity systems. Storm-2603, the actor behind Warlock ransomware, has been exploiting on-prem SharePoint since mid-2025, so this is also a ransomware precursor. Apply the May 2026 SharePoint update immediately, restrict and monitor authenticated access, and treat any exposed on-prem farm as a candidate pivot point toward OT-supporting IT.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: KEV-added 1 Jul 2026 — federal deadline 4 Jul 2026 (lapsed); exploitation ongoing

items.title: Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the invisible boxes that put legacy serial OT gear on the network
items.content_text: CISA added CVE-2025-67038 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on 23 June (federal deadline 26 June) after confirming in-the-wild exploitation: an unauthenticated attacker can inject OS commands through a username parameter on Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers and run code as root. The flaw is one of 20 “BRIDGE:BREAK” vulnerabilities Forescout disclosed in April across Lantronix and Silex serial-to-IP products, whose research showed attackers could manipulate sensor readings to conceal dangerous conditions in industrial and healthcare settings. The telling detail: Forescout's honeypot caught exploitation as early as 5 April — after Lantronix shipped the patch but before technical details were public — suggesting the attackers reverse-engineered the fix, with deliberate Lantronix-specific fingerprinting rather than botnet noise. These converters sit quietly in front of serial PLCs, meters and sensors at thousands of sites, and many are internet-exposed. Patch EDS5000 devices now, inventory every serial-to-IP converter in the estate, pull them off the public internet, and watch for anomalous outbound connections from device-server segments.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: KEV-added 23 Jun 2026 — exploitation confirmed; reported 25 Jun 2026

items.title: Unauthenticated API on ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals leaks device secrets — the VSAT gear that backhauls remote energy and transport sites
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-183-01 (2 July) covers two flaws in ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals used across communications, energy, defense, government and transportation. The more serious, CVE-2026-38059, is a missing-authentication issue: the terminal exposes REST API endpoints (/api/identity and others) with no authentication, so an unauthenticated attacker with network access can pull sensitive device data — serial number, Device ID, terminal private-key identifier, MAC address and exact firmware version — ideal reconnaissance for a follow-on attack. A companion CSRF flaw (CVE-2026-38057) lets an attacker force state-changing API calls against an authenticated session. VSAT terminals often provide the only connectivity for remote pipelines, substations, offshore and transport assets, so the device is frequently the network edge for OT. Update to firmware 4.5.2.2 or newer via the iDirect support portal, restrict management-network access to the terminals, and keep their APIs off any untrusted or internet-facing path.

items.title: NERC CIP-012-2 now in effect (1 July 2026) — tighter protection for control-center data
items.content_text: The revised NERC CIP-012-2 standard became enforceable on 1 July, strengthening requirements to protect the confidentiality and integrity of real-time assessment and monitoring data exchanged between control centers and closing gaps the earlier version left around how that inter-control-center traffic is secured. For bulk-electric-system operators it is an immediate compliance milestone; for everyone else it is a useful reminder that control-center-to-control-center links are a prime target and deserve encryption, integrity protection and monitoring regardless of regulatory scope.

items.title: Senate bill would force cybersecurity review — and possible recall — of Chinese-made networked medical devices in U.S. healthcare
items.content_text: Senator Tom Cotton introduced the Countering Chinese Cyberthreats for Patients (“Countering CCP”) Act, which would direct the FDA and CISA to retroactively review the cybersecurity of Chinese-made networked medical devices already deployed in U.S. healthcare. Manufacturers would have to disclose the software in their devices, their development processes and server locations; regulators would verify that U.S. patient data is not stored on or transmitted through China-based servers; and HHS could halt distribution of devices deemed risky — with recall for vendors that fail to provide the required information within three months. The bill follows the FDA's earlier warning that Contec CMS8000 patient monitors (also relabeled as Epsimed MN-120) shipped with a backdoor enabling remote access and data exfiltration. For critical-infrastructure operators beyond healthcare, it is another marker of where supply-chain scrutiny of foreign-made connected devices is heading — SBOM-style disclosure, data-flow verification and recall authority as regulatory tools. No action required yet; it is proposed legislation, not law.
